label: ai
topic: Students should openly express their political views and engage in debate on campus
text:
Watching the returns of the 2024 presidential election in my dormitory's common room was a profoundly surreal experience. As the electoral map on the television screen steadily bled red, a heavy, suffocating silence descended over the dozens of students gathered on the couches. Some classmates openly wept; others stared at their phones in disbelief, furiously typing panic-stricken messages into group chats. As a student attending a deeply liberal university, the grief and anxiety in the room were palpable and completely genuine. But as someone born and raised in a deep red, working-class county in the South, my feelings were far more complicated. I was watching my current world mourn a reality that my home world was simultaneously celebrating.

Navigating a liberal college campus as a student from a conservative background often feels like walking a tightrope without a net. At home, the people I love—my family, my high school teachers, my neighbors—are farmers, mechanics, and small-business owners. They go to church on Sundays, they own firearms, and they vote red because of deep-seated concerns about the economy, agricultural regulations, and traditional values that are fundamental to their way of life. Yet, in the seminar rooms of my university, these same people are frequently reduced to harsh, one-dimensional caricatures. I have sat quietly through sociology and political science lectures where conservative voters are dismissed wholesale as uneducated, hateful, or willfully ignorant. To speak up and offer nuance, to explain the very real economic anxieties driving the political choices in my hometown, feels like an invitation to immediate social ostracization.

Consequently, a quiet self-censorship takes hold. Conservative or moderate students at liberal universities learn very quickly to keep their heads down, nod along with the prevailing campus consensus in class, and save their true political opinions for whispered phone calls home. But this silence is profoundly damaging to everyone involved. When my liberal peers exist in an intellectual echo chamber where their views are constantly validated and never genuinely challenged, they are being robbed of a complete education. How can we possibly expect to heal a deeply fractured nation when the supposed future leaders of the country cannot even stomach a conversation with someone who votes differently than they do?

The 2024 election should serve as a massive wake-up call to our campus culture. The political reality of the United States cannot be wished away by curating our social circles and syllabus readings to reflect only one ideological perspective. We have to learn how to engage in respectful, face-to-face debate with people across the entire political spectrum, especially when it is deeply uncomfortable. This does not mean tolerating hate speech or abandoning our core moral compasses. It means acknowledging that intellectual friction is the very purpose of a university.

I challenge my conservative peers to be braver. We must speak up in class, share our lived perspectives, and endure the inevitable pushback. We cannot complain about being misunderstood by the coastal elite if we refuse to actually explain ourselves. Similarly, I challenge my liberal peers to replace immediate moral judgment with genuine curiosity. The next time a classmate expresses a political view that makes you bristle, resist the urge to mentally cancel them or attack their character. Instead, ask them how they arrived at that conclusion. Ask them about the community they came from and the experiences that shaped their worldview. You do not have to agree with them—in fact, you can and should argue passionately for your own beliefs—but you must engage with them as complex human beings.

We are currently preparing to graduate into a country that is bitterly divided, governed by mutual distrust and profound misunderstanding. If we cannot learn to bridge that divide within the relatively safe, intellectual confines of a college campus, we stand absolutely no chance of doing so in the real world. The 2024 election proved that half the country thinks fundamentally differently than the dominant voice on our campus. We can either plug our ears and continue to look the other way, or we can finally pull up a chair, lean into the discomfort, and actually start talking to one another.
